description: Filesystem kernel functions for I/O observability

answers: which files is a process opening
probes.use_when: you need to see which files a process is touching. fexit gives you the return value — ENOENT means file not found, EACCES means permission denied.
probes.not_when: you need I/O throughput — use vfs_read/vfs_write for bytes transferred.

interpretations.conclusion: file not found — the path does not exist
interpretations.action: check if the expected file exists. may be a missing configmap mount or wrong path.

interpretations.conclusion: permission denied — the process does not have access to this file
interpretations.action: check file permissions and securityContext. the container may need a different UID or fsGroup.

answers: which process is writing to disk
is a process writing too much
probes.use_when: you need per-process write throughput. high frequency — use sampling.
probes.not_when: you just need to know if a file was accessed — use vfs_open.

interpretations.pattern: high bytes per call from one process
interpretations.conclusion: heavy writer — this process is generating significant disk I/O
interpretations.action: normal for databases and log writers. investigate if unexpected.

answers: which process is reading from disk
is a process reading too much
probes.use_when: you need per-process read throughput. high frequency — use sampling. pair with vfs_write for complete I/O picture.
probes.not_when: you just need to know if a file was accessed — use vfs_open.

interpretations.pattern: high bytes per call from one process
interpretations.conclusion: heavy reader — this process is generating significant read I/O
interpretations.action: normal for databases and log processors. investigate if unexpected.

interpretations.conclusion: read error — the kernel returned an error for this read operation
interpretations.action: check filesystem health. may indicate disk errors or corrupt filesystem.

answers: which process is closing file descriptors
probes.use_when: you want to track file descriptor lifecycle. combine with vfs_open to detect fd leaks — opens without corresponding closes.
probes.not_when: you need I/O throughput — use vfs_read/vfs_write.

interpretations.pattern: close rate much lower than open rate for same process
interpretations.conclusion: possible file descriptor leak — process is opening files faster than closing them
interpretations.action: check application for fd leaks. compare open vs close rates over time.
